claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hi, thanks for helping me with this! My child Alex is 17, so that would be the 16 to 19 category.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're welcome! Happy to help you with this.

Since Alex is 17 (in the 16-19 age category), I need to check a few more things about their situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For young people aged 16-19 like Alex, Child Benefit eligibility depends on their education or training status.

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current situation?**

1. **In education or training** - Alex is currently enrolled in a course or approved training programme
2. **Recently left education** - Alex has left education or training within the last few months
3. **Neither** - Alex is not in education/training and hasn't been recently

Which option applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is currently in education - he's studying for his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful to know! A-levels are a common qualification for young people Alex's age. Let me check the education level requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels are on the list of approved qualifications for Child Benefit purposes.

I just need to confirm a few more details about Alex's studies:

1. **Is Alex studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study/classes)

2. **Is the course funded by an employer**, or has Alex agreed to work for an employer after completing the course as a condition of the training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is studying full-time at college - definitely more than 12 hours a week of classes and supervised study. And no, it's not funded by an employer or anything like that - it's just regular sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That all sounds good. A-levels at a regular sixth form college, full-time study, and no employer funding - Alex's education meets the approved criteria.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about Alex's work situation:

**Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week?** (Or is he not in paid work at all?)

This is important because young people aged 16+ who work 24 hours or more per week aren't eligible for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually Alex does work quite a bit - he has a part-time job at the local supermarket and works full-time hours there alongside his studies. So yes, he does work 24 hours or more per week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. That's quite impressive that Alex is balancing full-time studies with so many work hours!

However, this does affect the Child Benefit eligibility. Let me navigate to the outcome based on this information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to share some disappointing news. Based on the information you've provided, let me give you the outcome:

---

**Result: NOT ELIGIBLE**

**Reason:** Alex is 16 or over and is working 24 or more hours per week. Unfortunately, this means you cannot currently claim Child Benefit for him.

---

**Important Information:**

Even though Alex is in approved education (A-levels), the rule about working 24+ hours per week still applies to young people aged 16 and over.

**However, there is good news:**

- If Alex **reduces his working hours to below 24 hours per week** while continuing his studies, you may become eligible to claim Child Benefit again
- If his work hours change in the future (for example, during exam periods), it would be worth reassessing your eligibility

**What you can do:**
- Consider whether Alex could reduce his hours at the supermarket to under 24 per week
- If circumstances change, you can start a new Child Benefit claim

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about this, or do you have any other children you'd like me to assess for Child Benefit eligibility?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I've recorded the assessment outcome. Please don't hesitate to reach out if Alex's circumstances change or if you have any other questions!

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old (in the 16-19 age category). While he is in approved non-advanced education (studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college, with more than 12 hours per week of supervised study, and not employer-funded), he is working 24 or more hours per week at a part-time job. The rules state that children aged 16+ who work 24 or more hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, regardless of their education status.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 17), who is studying A-levels full-time. Although Alex meets the education requirements for Child Benefit, he works 24+ hours per week at a supermarket, which disqualifies him under the working hours rule for 16-19 year olds. The claimant could become eligible if Alex reduces his working hours to below 24 per week.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.reason: Child aged 16+ is working 24 or more hours per week and not in approved education or training
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops if child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24+ hours per week and is no longer in approved education or training. If they return to education/training, you may be able to claim again.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old (in the 16-19 age category). While he is in approved non-advanced education (studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college, with more than 12 hours per week of supervised study, and not employer-funded), he is working 24 or more hours per week at a part-time job. The rules state that children aged 16+ who work 24 or more hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, regardless of their education status.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 17), who is studying A-levels full-time. Although Alex meets the education requirements for Child Benefit, he works 24+ hours per week at a supermarket, which disqualifies him under the working hours rule for 16-19 year olds. The claimant could become eligible if Alex reduces his working hours to below 24 per week.
